impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder's conditioning of patent licenses on customers' exclusive purchase of unpatented products constitutes unlawful tying under the Sherman Act, and whether the unpatented products must have substantial non-infringing uses to sustain an antitrust claim.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed a jury verdict finding Ingevity liable for unlawful tying under the Sherman Act for conditioning licenses to its fuel vapor canister patent on customers' exclusive purchase of Ingevity's unpatented carbon honeycomb products. The court upheld that whether the tied products had substantial non-infringing uses was properly a jury question and affirmed the damages award.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors facing exclusionary patent-license tying arrangements now have strong appellate authority supporting antitrust claims and damages recovery against patent holders who leverage patents to foreclose competition in adjacent unpatented product markets.
impacts.negativeImplications: Companies that bundle patent licenses with requirements to purchase unpatented products face significant antitrust liability risk. The decision confirms that tying arrangements leveraging patent rights can violate the Sherman Act even where the tied product is related to the patent.

impacts.caseName: Global Tubing LLC v. Tenaris Coiled Tubes LLC
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether genuine disputes of material fact precluded summary judgment on both inequitable conduct in obtaining patents and a Walker Process antitrust fraud claim for attempted monopolization.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated summary judgment on both inequitable conduct (invalidating patents) and a Walker Process fraud claim (attempted monopolization through fraud on the patent office). The court found genuine disputes of material fact on both issues and remanded for trial, keeping alive both the patent enforceability defense and the antitrust counterclaim.
impacts.positiveImplications: Competitors accused of patent infringement have reinforced ability to pursue Walker Process antitrust counterclaims where evidence suggests the patent holder obtained patents through fraud on the PTO, potentially recovering treble damages.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face increased exposure to antitrust liability where prior art known to them was not disclosed during prosecution. The decision lowers the bar for Walker Process claims to survive summary judgment.

impacts.caseName: Trustees of Columbia University v. Gen Digital Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims directed to detecting anomalous program executions by comparing function calls in an emulator to a model are directed to an abstract idea under Alice step one, and whether damages may include sales to customers outside the United States.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit vacated a $185 million infringement judgment, holding that Columbia's cybersecurity patent claims were abstract at Alice step one and remanding for step two analysis. The court also ruled the district court erred in permitting damages based on foreign sales. However, it upheld the infringement and willfulness findings should the claims survive on remand.
impacts.positiveImplications: Technology companies defending against cybersecurity and software patent assertions gain another significant precedent narrowing patent eligibility under § 101, and the foreign-sales damages ruling limits patentees' ability to recover royalties on extraterritorial revenue.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders in the software and cybersecurity space face heightened risk that their claims will be found abstract under Alice. Universities and research institutions with software patents face reduced damages potential due to the extraterritoriality ruling.

impacts.caseName: GoTV Streaming, LLC v. Netflix, Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether patent claims covering a server that tailors content delivery specifications to a wireless device's screen size and capabilities are ineligible for patenting under 35 U.S.C. § 101.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that GoTV's patents claiming methods and systems for tailoring streamed content specifications to a requesting device's capabilities are ineligible under § 101, reversing the district court's judgment for GoTV on the '715 patent and requiring entry of judgment for Netflix. The court found the claims directed to an abstract idea of adapting content to device capabilities.
impacts.positiveImplications: Streaming and technology companies gain strong precedent that patents claiming generic server-side content adaptation to device capabilities are patent-ineligible, reducing exposure to infringement suits by patent assertion entities in the content delivery space.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders with claims directed to content adaptation and delivery optimization face increased vulnerability to § 101 challenges, particularly where claims recite functional results without sufficiently specific technical implementation.

impacts.caseName: VLSI Technology LLC v. Intel Corporation
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a patent holder may recover damages for infringement of a multi-core processor patent where the accused method steps occur partly outside the United States, and whether the district court properly granted summary judgment on extraterritoriality and doctrine of equivalents grounds.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed in part the district court's grant of summary judgment of noninfringement, finding that VLSI raised genuine issues of material fact regarding Intel's infringement of the '836 patent under the doctrine of equivalents. The court also addressed extraterritoriality issues in method patent claims where some steps occur abroad, and reversed the striking of VLSI's damages expert theories.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders asserting method claims against global technology companies benefit from the court's willingness to allow doctrine of equivalents theories and damages expert testimony to proceed to trial, even where literal infringement is disputed.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies with global operations face continued uncertainty about extraterritorial reach of U.S. method patents and exposure to damages claims that the district court had previously excluded.

impacts.caseName: Ascendis Pharma A/S v. BioMarin Pharmaceutical Inc.
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether an ITC respondent that missed the statutory deadline for seeking a mandatory stay of a district court action under 28 U.S.C. § 1659(a)(2) may obtain such a stay by voluntarily dismissing and refiling the same declaratory judgment action.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit held that BioMarin could not circumvent the statutory 30-day deadline for seeking a mandatory stay of a parallel district court patent action by dismissing and refiling a new declaratory judgment action. The court found this tactic impermissible because it would render the statutory deadlines meaningless.
impacts.positiveImplications: Parties filing declaratory judgment actions in district court while facing parallel ITC proceedings gain certainty that their litigation cannot be indefinitely delayed by an opponent's strategic refiling to reset mandatory stay deadlines.
impacts.negativeImplications: ITC respondents who miss the § 1659(a)(2) deadline lose the ability to obtain a mandatory stay through refiling tactics, potentially forcing them to litigate simultaneously in the ITC and district court.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether a Rule 30(b)(6) corporate representative may testify about historical product sales outside their personal knowledge for purposes of establishing the on-sale bar under 35 U.S.C. § 102(b), and whether source code constitutes hearsay.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed summary judgment of invalidity under the pre-AIA on-sale bar, holding that a 30(b)(6) corporate witness may testify about historical sales even without personal knowledge, and that source code consisting of computer commands is not hearsay. The court found that prior sales of a device embodying all claim limitations more than one year before the priority date invalidated the patent.
impacts.positiveImplications: Accused infringers benefit from expanded ability to prove on-sale bar invalidity through 30(b)(6) testimony about historical corporate sales and direct use of source code evidence, even when original engineers are unavailable.
impacts.negativeImplications: Patent holders face greater vulnerability to on-sale bar challenges where predecessor companies sold potentially anticipating products, as corporate representatives can now testify about such sales without personal knowledge.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC correctly found that Apple Watch models with blood oxygen measurement features infringed Masimo's patents covering wearable pulse oximetry devices, and whether the limited exclusion order was properly issued.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the ITC's finding that Apple violated § 337 by importing Apple Watch models infringing Masimo's wearable blood oxygen measurement patents, upholding the limited exclusion order barring importation. The court found no error in the Commission's domestic industry determination, validity rulings, or infringement findings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent holders with domestic industry investments in wearable health technology have strong precedent for obtaining ITC exclusion orders against major technology companies importing infringing products.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies incorporating patented health-monitoring features into consumer devices face significant import ban risk through ITC proceedings, potentially requiring product redesigns or licensing agreements to maintain market access.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court abused its discretion in excluding a patent plaintiff's damages expert report and granting summary judgment based on absence of a remedy.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the district court's exclusion of Exafer's damages expert report and vacated summary judgment for Microsoft based on absence of a remedy. The court found the district court applied an overly rigid standard in excluding the expert's reasonable royalty analysis, holding that the identified flaws went to weight rather than admissibility.
impacts.positiveImplications: Patent plaintiffs benefit from a more permissive standard for admissibility of damages expert testimony, as the court clarified that methodological criticisms generally affect weight rather than admissibility under Daubert.
impacts.negativeImplications: Accused infringers like Microsoft lose the ability to obtain dispositive rulings based on exclusion of damages experts where the expert applied a recognized methodology, even if specific inputs or assumptions are debatable.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the PTO Director's instructions to the Board governing discretionary denials of inter partes review petitions are 'general statements of policy' exempt from APA notice-and-comment rulemaking requirements.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the PTO Director's instructions governing discretionary IPR non-institution decisions are general statements of policy exempt from notice-and-comment rulemaking under 5 U.S.C. § 553(b). The court emphasized that Congress provided no legal right to IPR institution and that the Director retains ultimate authority over non-institution decisions.
impacts.positiveImplications: The PTO retains broad flexibility to issue and modify IPR institution guidance without formal rulemaking, providing institutional efficiency in managing patent review proceedings.
impacts.negativeImplications: Technology companies challenging patents through IPRs cannot compel notice-and-comment rulemaking for PTO institution policies, leaving them with limited recourse when discretionary denials increase under policy guidance changes.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the district court properly approved a $125 million class action settlement of claims that the federal judiciary overcharged PACER users by using fees to fund expenses unrelated to PACER operations, including the propriety of attorneys' fees and incentive awards to class representatives.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed the district court's approval of a $125 million settlement in a nationwide class action alleging the federal judiciary exceeded statutory authority by using PACER fees to fund unrelated expenses. The court upheld attorneys' fees awards and incentive awards to the three nonprofit class representatives, rejecting an objector's challenges.
impacts.positiveImplications: The settlement provides substantial reimbursement to hundreds of thousands of PACER users, including law firms and businesses, and establishes that incentive awards to organizational class representatives remain permissible in appropriate circumstances.

impacts.caseName: 112 Genesee Street, LLC v. United States
impacts.legalQuestion: Whether over 300 restaurants denied Restaurant Revitalization Fund grants despite timely applications stated a viable Tucker Act claim for damages against the United States based on the SBA's failure to process applications in the statutorily mandated order.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that restaurants and businesses denied RRF grants could pursue Tucker Act damages claims, holding that the statute's mandatory language ('shall award grants' in the order received) created money-mandating obligations. The SBA's processing of later applicants ahead of earlier ones violated the statutory mandate.
impacts.positiveImplications: Small businesses that applied for but were denied pandemic relief grants due to agency processing errors have a viable path to recover damages from the government, establishing important precedent for enforcing statutory grant distribution requirements.
impacts.negativeImplications: The government faces potential significant liability for failure to distribute pandemic relief funds in the statutorily required order, which could encourage more claims challenging agency administration of congressionally mandated spending programs.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether the ITC's practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses in antidumping and countervailing duty investigations as confidential is consistent with the governing statute and the common law right of access to judicial records.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit affirmed that the ITC's blanket practice of automatically designating all questionnaire responses as confidential in trade investigations is incompatible with the statute, which requires individualized confidentiality determinations. The court held that 19 U.S.C. § 1516a does not abrogate the common law right of public access to judicial proceedings.
impacts.positiveImplications: Domestic industries and the public gain greater transparency in international trade proceedings, as companies can access questionnaire data that was previously automatically shielded from disclosure, potentially strengthening trade remedy cases.
impacts.negativeImplications: Foreign exporters and importers responding to ITC questionnaires face increased risk that their commercially sensitive business information will become publicly accessible, potentially discouraging full cooperation with trade investigations.

impacts.legalQuestion: Whether federal nurses employed by the Indian Health Service under Title 5 but paid under Title 38's special salary provisions are entitled to enhanced compensation rates for overtime, nighttime, Sunday, and holiday work under Title 38.
impacts.description: The Federal Circuit reversed the Court of Federal Claims' dismissal of claims by IHS registered nurses seeking enhanced pay for overtime, nighttime, Sunday, and holiday work under 38 U.S.C. § 7453. The court held that IHS nurses paid under Title 38's base rate provisions stated viable claims for the additional compensation benefits available under that title, potentially affecting all similarly situated current and former IHS nurses.
impacts.negativeImplications: Federal healthcare agencies face potential class-wide backpay liability for failing to apply Title 38 compensation enhancements to Title 5 healthcare employees who receive Title 38 base pay, creating significant fiscal exposure for agencies employing nurses under hybrid pay frameworks.
